lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: February. Twenty-two hundred hours. The ambulance bay at Mercy General sits under a sky the colour of a bruise, the kind of cold that doesn't announce itself — it simply arrives and stays.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: A set of headlights swings through the gate. Rig Seven. The engine idles a second too long before the driver cuts it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo Okafor stands at the bay desk with a clipboard, a paper coffee cup going cold beside his elbow. He runs a finger down the night's manifest — transfers, walk-ins, the roster of rigs on rotation — and stops.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He says the name the way you test ice: not aloud enough for anyone else to hear, just loud enough to make it real.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The bay doors roll open. Cold air floods in ahead of her — a wall of February that smells of exhaust and something sharper underneath, something medicinal. Dana Voss steps through, her gear bag over one shoulder, pulling a stretcher with her free hand. Her partner, a kid Theo doesn't recognise, takes the other end. Their patient is stable. The vitals monitor beeps at a slow, reassuring interval.

lines.speaker: Dana Voss
lines.text: Mercy General? Where do you want him?

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She doesn't look at him. Not deliberately — she's already moving, already giving the handoff summary to the triage nurse who materialises at her elbow. It's the efficiency of someone who's done this a thousand times, in a hundred different bays, who can run the protocol on autopilot while her mind is somewhere else entirely.

lines.speaker: Dana Voss
lines.text: Fifty-one-year-old male, single-vehicle, restrained driver, airbag deployment, GCS fourteen on scene, dropped to twelve in transit, BP one-ten over seventy and trending down. Possible internal. He needs a belly scan now, not in twenty minutes.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The stretcher wheels clatter over the threshold. Theo watches the whole thing from the desk, his pen hovering over the manifest box where he still needs to mark her in.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He writes the time. He draws the small tick beside her name that means arrived, present, accounted for. His eyes drift to the corridor beyond the bay — the long stretch of floor leading to the trauma rooms, to the locker hall, to the half-dozen places Maeve Callahan might be right now.

lines.speaker: Theo Okafor
lines.style: quiet, as if answering a question no one asked
lines.text: She's on a consult. Third floor.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He's not talking to anyone. The bay is already half-empty again, Dana and her partner pushing through to bay four, the doors swinging behind them. The cold lingers a moment longer than they do.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo sets down his pen. He picks up his coffee. He looks at the manifest — Voss, D., Rig Seven, 22:07 — and then he looks at the overnight board, where the pile-up call hasn't come in yet, where the night still looks, from this angle, almost manageable.
